summary: Julia Evans reflects on her decade-long journey learning to program, pushing back against the notion that programming can be learned quickly. She traces her path from writing BASIC on a TI-83 calculator in 2004 through university and into professional work, noting that progress was slow and nonlinear. Looking back at her old code, she was surprised not by its quality but by how little she had actually written in those early years. The post serves as reassurance to beginners that learning to program is a long process, and that even experienced developers spent years feeling uncertain about their abilities.
key_points: Learning to program took roughly 10 years of mostly low-speed progress before she felt confident, contradicting narratives about learning in weeks or months
Looking back at early code, the most surprising thing wasn't its quality but how little of it there was — she simply didn't write many programs in those first years
Working 40 hours a week as a professional programmer taught her more about software engineering than years of casual scripting
She didn't consider herself good at programming even after completing math/CS degrees, and never thought to apply to prestigious tech companies
money_quotes: I don't generally like giving advice, because I think it's way too easy to generalize your own experience into advice and do it wrong.
I spent about 10 years learning to program before going to Hacker School, mostly at fairly low speeds.
I was amazed to find out you could write subroutines and define your own variable names because on the TI-83 you only have about 26 variables (A-Z)
At this point (2011) I still basically don't believe I'm any good at programming. I do not in any way consider applying to fancy jobs at Google or Microsoft or whatever.
What was the most surprising was how little of it I found.
If you're programming and just starting out, and worrying that people say you can learn to program in 24 hours or 12 weeks… whatever. It's taken me 11 years so far, and I'm not done :)
key_insight: Learning to program is a years-long journey of slow, uneven progress, and the pressure to learn quickly in weeks or months doesn't match the reality of how most programmers actually develop their skills.
